planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 3–4 weeks before transplanting; sow after last frost in warm soil; a traditional Italian heirloom from the Chioggia coastal region near Venice that stores exceptionally well into winter.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, fertile soil after last frost, or start 3–4 weeks early indoors; plant in hills of 2–3 seeds per mound spaced 5–6 feet apart and thin to the strongest plant. Marina di Chioggia is a prized Italian heirloom winter squash from the Chioggia lagoon region, producing large 8–15 pound fruits with deeply ridged, blue-green skin covered in grey-green bumps and warts, and brilliant orange flesh that is dense, sweet, nutty, and nearly stringless — among the finest-flavored winter squashes. Cure fruits for 2 weeks in a warm, dry location after harvest to harden the skin and concentrate the sugars; properly cured fruits store for 4–6 months. The large vines need ample space; train them onto a trellis or let them sprawl in open ground.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; this Southern Appalachian heirloom has been grown in the mountains of North Carolina and Tennessee for generations and is one of the best-flavored large winter squashes for warm-climate gardeners.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in a prepared hill of well-composted soil after last frost, planting 3 seeds and thinning to the strongest; Candy Roaster needs a long season of 90–110 days, so in zone 9–11 plant in late winter for a spring harvest or late summer for fall. Candy Roaster is a Southern Appalachian heirloom producing large, elongated, banana-shaped fruits 12–20 inches long with pinkish-tan to salmon-orange skin often tipped with blue-green at the stem end, and sweet, orange, dry-textured flesh that roasts to a concentrated, candy-like sweetness the name describes perfectly. Fruits can reach 15–30 pounds; cure for 2 weeks in warm, dry conditions and store for 3–5 months. An essential heirloom for gardeners who want an authentic piece of Appalachian foodway heritage.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in fertile, well-amended soil; one of the oldest American heirloom squashes, documented in New England gardens since the 1830s, with vibrant orange-red skin and superb flavor.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, enriched soil, spacing hills 4–6 feet apart and thinning to 2 plants per hill; Boston Marrow requires 90–100 days of warm weather, so plant promptly after last frost. Boston Marrow is one of the oldest American heirloom winter squashes, offered by commercial seedsmen in New England since the 1830s; it produces round to pear-shaped fruits 6–10 pounds in weight with spectacular, deep orange-red skin and extremely sweet, fine-grained, nearly stringless flesh of exceptional quality — it was reportedly the standard squash for pies before Hubbard types became popular. Harvest when the rind is hard and the skin is fully colored; cure for 10 days and store in a cool, dry place for 2–3 months.

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; an 1898 introduction and one of the most widely grown Hubbard types, with brilliant red-orange skin and an easier-to-cut size than the giant Blue Hubbard.

directions: Sow 2–3 seeds per hill 1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 1–2 plants once true leaves appear and keep hills evenly moist through fruit set. Golden Hubbard was introduced around 1898 as a smaller, easier-to-handle alternative to the large blue Hubbard types, producing teardrop-shaped fruits 6–10 pounds in weight with vivid orange-red, bumpy skin and dense, sweet, yellow-orange flesh with excellent dry texture ideal for baking and puréeing. Harvest after the first light frost has killed the vines and the rind resists puncture with a fingernail; cure in a 70–85°F location for 10–14 days then store at 50–60°F for up to 4 months. The relatively manageable fruit size makes it more practical for home cooks than the 15–20 pound Blue Hubbard.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in fertile, deeply amended soil; needs a long season of 100–115 days; this is the classic 'True Hubbard' type with heavily warted, dark blue-green skin and exceptional 6-month storage life.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil after last frost, in hills enriched with compost, spacing 5–7 feet apart; thin to 2 plants per hill and provide consistent moisture through the long growing season. True Hubbard is the heavily warted, dark blue-green Hubbard squash that was a staple of 19th-century New England kitchens; fruits weigh 10–20 pounds and have extremely hard, deeply ribbed and warted blue-green skin and dense, dry, bright orange flesh with outstanding sweet, nutty flavor. The exceptional storage quality — fruits in good condition can last 6 months or more at cool temperatures — made it the premier cellar squash before canning became widespread. Harvest only after the vine has fully died back and the rind cannot be dented with a thumbnail; cure for 2 weeks in a warm, airy spot before moving to cold storage.
